sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated dissolution of an ionic salt in a coffee-cup calorimeter to estimate the sign and magnitude of heat exchange to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that solution temperature changes indicate heat absorbed or released by the dissolution process at constant pressure. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as qsolution = mcΔT and ΔHrxn is calculated from -qsolution per mole of solute. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved dissolving increasing moles of salt into equal masses of water at the same initial temperature. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: temperature decreased more when more salt dissolved, indicating an endothermic dissolution. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that water mass, calorimeter constant, stirring rate, and initial temperature were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that a temperature decrease means the dissolution reaction released heat into the solution. Another was that enthalpy per mole can be found from ΔT alone without considering mass or moles. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to dissolution calorimetry, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the dissolution calorimetry experiment?

choices.A: The observed cooling is best interpreted as heat absorbed by the dissolving process from the solution
choices.B: A temperature decrease means the dissolution reaction released heat into the solution
choices.C: Enthalpy per mole can be found from δt alone without considering mass or moles, even though the passage states that water mass was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

explanations.A: Correct. This choice fits the manipulation, the graph, and the controls simultaneously. The passage gives the governing relationship as qsolution = mcΔT and ΔHrxn is calculated from -qsolution per mole of solute, so the relevant reasoning is not just recognizing a topic word but applying the dependency in that relationship. Here, temperature decreased more when more salt dissolved, indicating an endothermic dissolution, while the investigators verified that water mass, calorimeter constant, stirring rate, and initial temperature were controlled. That supports this answer because The observed cooling is best interpreted as heat absorbed by the dissolving process from the solution. It also preserves the direction of the effect and predicts the appropriate follow-up behavior if the manipulated variable were changed again. The other choices usually reverse causality, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore units, or explain the trend by changing a variable that the passage explicitly held constant.
explanations.B: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that A temperature decrease means the dissolution reaction released heat into the solution conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, qsolution = mcΔT and ΔHrxn is calculated from -qsolution per mole of solute, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that temperature decreased more when more salt dissolved, indicating an endothermic dissolution and also states that water mass, calorimeter constant, stirring rate, and initial temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.C: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that Enthalpy per mole can be found from δt alone without considering mass or moles, even though the passage states that water mass was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, qsolution = mcΔT and ΔHrxn is calculated from -qsolution per mole of solute, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that temperature decreased more when more salt dissolved, indicating an endothermic dissolution and also states that water mass, calorimeter constant, stirring rate, and initial temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.D: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, qsolution = mcΔT and ΔHrxn is calculated from -qsolution per mole of solute, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that temperature decreased more when more salt dissolved, indicating an endothermic dissolution and also states that water mass, calorimeter constant, stirring rate, and initial temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.

choices.B: The graph shows no systematic change when dissolving increasing moles of salt into equal masses of water at the same initial temperature
choices.C: The measured response changes only after a controlled variable is deliberately altered
choices.D: The plotted trend is opposite to the direction predicted by the stated relationship

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: Calorimetry uses conservation of energy to relate solution temperature change to reaction enthalpy
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that water mass was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a spirometer calibration syringe containing a fixed amount of gas compressed at constant temperature to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that for a fixed amount of ideal gas at constant temperature, pressure varies inversely with volume. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as PV = nRT, so P1V1 = P2V2 when n and T are constant. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved reducing gas volume while monitoring pressure after thermal equilibration. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: pressure increased approximately inversely with volume. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that gas amount, temperature bath, and sensor calibration were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that compressing a gas at constant temperature lowers pressure because molecules have less room to move. Another was that pressure depends only on moles and is independent of volume when temperature is fixed. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to spirometer gas compression, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion. The investigators emphasized that the interpretation depends on connecting the measured trend to the relevant physical relationship, the experimental controls, and the limiting behavior of the system rather than selecting a formula by keyword alone.

choices.B: The pressure-volume pattern is best explained by Boyle-law behavior from the ideal gas equation
choices.C: Pressure depends only on moles and is independent of volume when temperature is fixed
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls
